summary: The post argues that audience-based companies like Facebook, Twitter, and Snapchat function essentially as public goods but are forced into traditional for-profit models that require monetization through advertising and data collection. Using Wikipedia as a counterexample, Asparouhova suggests these platforms could have remained neutral communication utilities as nonprofits rather than pursuing IPOs. She examines emerging funding mechanisms—syndicates, crowdfunding, and nonprofit-friendly investment vehicles—that could enable valuable tech innovations to prioritize impact over billion-dollar exits. The piece concludes that funders hold the power to reshape incentives and create alternatives to the current system.
key_points: Audience companies like Facebook are essentially public goods (nonexcludable, nonrivalrous) that we've awkwardly forced into for-profit business models
Wikipedia chose nonprofit status to preserve neutrality—Facebook could have made the same choice but didn't, leading to data monetization
The current funding ecosystem only offers two exits (acquisition or IPO), constraining how valuable services can operate
New funding vehicles (AngelList Syndicates, crowdfunding platforms, nonprofit-friendly funds) are emerging that could support impact-focused companies
The responsibility for change lies with funders who write the checks, not startups chasing unicorn valuations
money_quotes: Having Facebook on a mobile phone is more important than being able to browse anything else on the web or use any other mobile app. It's a communication lifeline.
What if tech were valued in our society because it helps us build useful things for the world on the cheap, not because of its inflated valuations and perceived glamorous lifestyle?
In the offline world, not everything that provides value is a for-profit corporation. The online world, as it matures, will eventually follow suit.
It would be naive to assume that startups will learn to do the 'right thing' and not pursue their dreams of mega-billion unicorns. They're currently not being incentivized to do otherwise.
It's up to funders—those who are writing the checks—to take a step back and think about what kind of world they want to create.

This type of company didn't exist before the Internet, and arguably, not before 2002, with the launch of Friendster. We didn't have a set of rules for them yet, so we backdated them into our existing business rules.
key_insight: The structure of tech funding—not the nature of technology itself—forces valuable communication platforms into advertising-driven business models that compromise user privacy and neutrality.
